narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 65.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately led to the rejection of your application—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to ensure student success. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, meaning you were in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range 5th or 6th decile, stood out as the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, suggesting the model sees this as a strong signal of struggle ahead. This outweighed positives like your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, which is a bit below the average 3.21 but helped pull the odds slightly in your favor. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1st nudged things toward failure, though less intensely than Year 3, indicating early academic challenges carried weight. Your LSAT score of 32, lower than the usual 36.7 average, also tempered the prediction positively but couldn't overcome the top concerns. Being full-time, like most applicants at 92.7%, offered a small boost toward passing, while your middle family income aligned closely with averages and had minimal sway. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower deciles and scores place you below the stronger profiles the model favors for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your metrics stacked up. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or boosting your GPA through post-bac courses to shift those key academic signals, and target schools with holistic reviews that value your full story. You're capable of turning this around; many do with focused preparation. Keep pushing—we're rooting for you.
